title: My definitive review of Fallen Land 2nd edition (w/ expansion, from the solo perspective)
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=SOXoRwvxcv0

The capstone. Daniel (@dungeondive) sits down for his definitive Fallen Land 2nd Edition solo review and, in passing, reveals that the game is going to land on his upcoming top-10 adventure games of all time. Seventeen years of design (2005 → 2022), and it shows on the table.

He plays a long detailed mid-game state — the Brotherhood out of St George, Utah, against a ghost AI opponent — and uses it to demonstrate the game's bones rather than just describe them. We see the armoured car and Bruce Lee's nunchucks loadout, the linked-gear bonuses chaining four cards into a stacked combat advantage, the homemade BGG-published character/town sheet that absorbs the bookkeeping, and the strict house rule that *if you forget a bonus you lose the bonus* — managing your faction is part of the design, not a sideshow.

Two example turns walk through the new end-of-turn town-events phase from Descendants (a Protectorate event, a 40-town-health threshold trigger), a Plains encounter against cattle rustlers won on overwhelming combat and survival rolls, a resource takeover, and a bookkeeping-heavy mountain breakdown. Throughout, Daniel keeps returning to the same verdict: this is one of the finest adventure games ever made, the documentation is unmatched, and his criticisms barely qualify — fiddly bookkeeping (solved by the sheet), text-heavy cards (don't play with six), and the small one of the criticisms is having to wear his glasses.

----

Daniel ends saying he can't imagine the Dungeon Dive collection without Fallen Land 2nd Edition — a permanent-shelf game. Which game in your collection has crossed that line for you, where downsizing or a cull simply wouldn't apply?
